claims.text: The Einstein field equations are R_{μν} - (1/2)R g_{μν} + Λ g_{μν} = (8πG/c⁴) T_{μν}.
We assume a vanishing cosmological constant Λ = 0.
We seek a solution in vacuum (T_{μν} = 0) exterior to a spherically symmetric, nonrotating, uncharged mass M.
Vacuum implies R_{μν} = 0 (tracing the equations yields R = 0, so the Einstein tensor vanishes if and only if the Ricci tensor does).
By Birkhoff's theorem, the vacuum solution is necessarily static (time-independent with no dt dr cross terms).
Spherical symmetry further restricts the form of the line element to depend only on a radial coordinate r, with the angular sector reducing to the standard round 2-sphere.
Asymptotic flatness (Minkowski spacetime recovered as r → ∞) is also assumed.
The general ansatz is ds² = -A(r) c² dt² + B(r) dr² + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²), where A(r) → 1 and B(r) → 1 as r → ∞.

The nonvanishing Christoffel symbols for this metric are Γ^t_{tr} = A'/(2A), Γ^r_{tt} = c²A'/(2B), Γ^r_{rr} = B'/(2B), Γ^r_{θθ} = -r/B, Γ^r_{φφ} = -r sin²θ/B, Γ^θ_{rθ} = 1/r, Γ^φ_{rφ} = 1/r, Γ^φ_{θφ} = cotθ, Γ^θ_{φφ} = -sinθ cosθ.
The Ricci tensor is R_{μν} = ∂_λ Γ^λ_{μν} - ∂_ν Γ^λ_{μλ} + Γ^λ_{σλ} Γ^σ_{μν} - Γ^λ_{σν} Γ^σ_{μλ}.
The Einstein equations reduce to the independent conditions R_{tt} = 0, R_{rr} = 0, and R_{θθ} = 0.
The φφ component is proportional to the θθ component by spherical symmetry.
Off-diagonal components such as R_{tr} vanish identically by staticity.
claims.notes: Model later uses R_{tr}=0 as a nontrivial equation; inconsistency.

claims.text: First consider R_{tr} = 0. Substituting the Christoffel symbols yields ∂_r(A'/A + B'/B) = 0.
claims.notes: Model said R_{tr} vanishes identically, then uses it as an equation.

claims.text: Integration gives A(r)B(r) = K (constant).
claims.notes: ∂_r(ln A + ln B)=0 gives AB=const; correct logic.

claims.text: Asymptotic flatness forces K = 1, so B(r) = 1/A(r).
The metric simplifies to ds² = -A(r) c² dt² + dr²/A(r) + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).
Setting R_{θθ} = 0 with B = 1/A (so B' = -A'/A²) yields 1 - A - rA' = 0.
The equation 1 - A - rA' = 0 is equivalent to dA/(1-A) = dr/r.
Direct integration produces -ln|1-A| = ln r + C₁, implying 1 - A = C/r.
A(r) = 1 - C/r, where C > 0 is an integration constant.
The sign C > 0 ensures a sensible Newtonian limit.
The Schwarzschild metric is ds² = -(1 - C/r)c² dt² + dr²/(1 - C/r) + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).
The remaining equation R_{tt} = 0 or R_{rr} = 0 is automatically satisfied by this form.
For weak fields (|Φ| ≪ c²), g_{tt} ≈ -(1 + 2Φ/c²)c², so A(r) ≈ 1 + 2Φ/c².
Poisson's equation ∇²Φ = 4πGρ for a spherical mass M has solution Φ = -GM/r.
A(r) ≈ 1 - 2GM/(c²r), implying C = 2GM/c².
The Schwarzschild metric is ds² = -(1 - 2GM/(c²r))c² dt² + dr²/(1 - 2GM/(c²r)) + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).
A coordinate singularity occurs where the metric coefficients diverge or vanish, i.e., where A(r) = 0 or equivalently where the coefficient of dr² diverges.
The coordinate singularity is at r = r_s = 2GM/c².
This is a coordinate singularity, not a curvature singularity.
The Kretschner scalar R_{αβγδ}R^{αβγδ} remains finite as r → r_s.
claims.notes: Model writes 'Kretschner'; standard name is 'Kretschner' or 'Kretschmann'.

claims.text: The metric can be extended smoothly across r_s in other coordinate systems, e.g., Eddington–Finkelstein or Kruskal–Szekeres.
The Schwarzschild radius is r_s = 2GM/c².
The R_{tr} equation, after substituting Christoffel symbols, yields ∂_r(A'/A + B'/B) = 0.
claims.notes: R_{tr}=0 is actually automatic for diagonal static metric.

claims.text: The cancellations yielding the compact form of R_{θθ} include csc²θ - cot²θ = 1 from angular Christoffel symbols.
R_{θθ} = 0 holds identically for flat spacetime when A = B = 1.
